metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an acceptance criterion for the Section 3 bootstrap: e.g. 'when an identity has an `email` but no `{provider:"jira"}` mapping, the reconciler attempts `/rest/api/3/user/search?query=<email>`; a stubbed hit returns the accountId and uses it; a stubbed miss degrades to the existing search/unassigned path without failing the sync (test in acli_rest.py or outbound_differ integration fixture).'
findings.finding: Section 3 (best-effort `/user/search` bootstrap) is listed as in-scope in the What section and file_impact but has no corresponding acceptance criterion — making it unverifiable and potentially skippable without detection.
findings.location: ## What / Section 3; ## Acceptance Criteria (no AC for Section 3)
findings.suggested_fix:
Add to Acceptance Criteria:
- [ ] When an identity carries an `email` but no `{provider:"jira", external_id:…}` mapping, `acli_rest.py` issues a best-effort `/rest/api/3/user/search?query=<email>` at reconcile time; a stubbed single-result response causes the returned accountId to be used for assignment; a stubbed empty/error response falls through to the existing assignee-search path without hard-failing (assert via unit test on `acli_rest.py` with a mocked HTTP client).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `dispatch_one.py` and `outbound_fields.py` to `file_impact` (and the plan's What section) to cover the reporter field mapping and the `_OUTBOUND_BATCH_ALLOWLIST` addition required to prevent the reporter field being silently stripped.
findings.finding: The plan places reporter-by-accountId logic in `apply_handlers.py`, but the field mapping layer (`outbound_fields.py`) and the dispatch allowlist (`dispatch_one.py:_OUTBOUND_BATCH_ALLOWLIST`) are the true upstream owners of what fields reach Jira — neither is in `file_impact`. If 'reporter' is not added to `_OUTBOUND_BATCH_ALLOWLIST` it will be silently stripped before `apply_handlers` ever sees it.
findings.location: ## file_impact / Section 4 (Reporter by accountId where permitted)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/dispatch_one.py` to file_impact with reason 'add reporter to _OUTBOUND_BATCH_ALLOWLIST'; add `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/outbound_fields.py` with reason 'map local reporter field to Jira reporter field in _map_local_to_jira_fields'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `acli.py` to `file_impact` and specify how a pre-resolved accountId from `jira_account_id` bypasses the second `validate_assignee_exists` call in `update_issue` (e.g., by accepting an already-validated accountId sentinel or by early-returning when the accountId is already an exact accountId string).
findings.finding: `acli.update_issue` (line 354) unconditionally re-resolves any non-empty assignee via `validate_assignee_exists` — so even when `_assignee_resolver` fast-paths with a Jira accountId via `jira_account_id`, `update_issue` will submit that accountId as the query to `/user/assignable/search` a SECOND time. This unenumerated consumer could degrade the fast-path benefit or cause a miss if the accountId is not returned by the fuzzy `query=` search.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify Section 4's error-detection contract: specify whether the reporter is SET in a separate REST sub-call (so its 400 can be isolated) or whether the field-level errors JSON body is parsed to distinguish `errors.reporter` from a full 403 token-permission failure — and update the AC to require a test covering the 403-on-full-update case.
findings.finding: Section 4's reporter permission detection asserts that `403/400` on the reporter field means 'not granted' and should be swallowed. In Jira Cloud REST API v3, a restricted-field error inside a multi-field PUT (`/rest/api/3/issue/{key}`) is returned as HTTP 400 with a JSON body containing a field-level errors object (e.g. `{"errors":{"reporter":"Field 'reporter' cannot be set."}}`), NOT as a 403. A 403 on that same call means the token lacks issue-edit permission entirely — swallowing it would silently skip ALL updates, not just the reporter field. The plan conflates these two distinct error shapes.
findings.suggested_fix: Set reporter in a dedicated sub-call (`PUT /rest/api/3/issue/{key}` with only `{fields:{reporter:{accountId:...}}}`) so a 400/403 is unambiguously reporter-specific and can be swallowed without affecting other field mutations.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC for the §3 best-effort /user/search bootstrap: specify the success case (email resolves to accountId, used for this reconcile run), the graceful-degradation case (hidden email / no result → existing path, no failure), and whether the resolved accountId is ephemeral or written back to the identity store.
findings.finding: Section 3's best-effort /user/search bootstrap is declared IN scope and listed in file_impact (acli_rest.py) but has no covering acceptance criterion — it is an orphan deliverable.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '- [ ] When an identity has an email but no {provider:"jira"} accountId mapping, a best-effort /rest/api/3/user/search?query=<email> lookup attempts to resolve the accountId at reconcile-time; if it succeeds the accountId is used for this run; if it fails (no result, privacy-hidden email, network error) the reconciler continues on the existing search/unassigned path without failing (test: stubbed search hit → accountId used; stubbed search miss → graceful degradation, no exception).' Then clarify whether the resolved accountId is written back or remains ephemeral.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC (and corresponding stubbed-client test) for the `/user/search` bootstrap covering: (a) email resolves to a unique accountId, and (b) email is hidden / returns no result → degrades to existing path without failing the sync.
findings.finding: The /user/search bootstrap (Section 3, acli_rest.py) introduces a new network-boundary behavior with its own failure modes (privacy-hidden email returns no result, empty result set, multiple ambiguous results) but no Acceptance Criterion or test covers it — neither the happy path (email resolves to an accountId) nor the graceful-degradation path (email hidden or no result).
findings.location: Section 3 ('`/user/search` bootstrap') and Acceptance Criteria block
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ACs: '`/user/search` bootstrap: when an identity has an email but no jira accountId mapping, a stubbed `/user/search` response returning a single match causes the reconciler to use that accountId; a stubbed empty/error response causes graceful fallback to the existing search/unassigned path (no exception raised, no sync abort). Multiple-match behavior is defined (e.g., skip bootstrap, treat as no-result).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify the exact HTTP status code and response body shape Jira v3 returns when a service account lacks Modify-Reporter permission (field-level error: HTTP 400 with `{"errors":{"reporter":...}}` vs. top-level HTTP 403), and constrain the swallow logic to the specific field-error key rather than a bare 400/403 catch.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that Modify-Reporter permission denial is detectable via '403/400 on the reporter field' (Section 4), but Jira REST API v3 returns field-level permission/validation errors as HTTP 400 with a structured `errors` body (keyed by field name) — not a top-level 403. A bare HTTP 400 swallow could mask genuine malformed-payload errors (wrong accountId shape, missing required fields) as 'permission not granted', silently discarding real bugs. This capability assertion is unverified against the Jira v3 spec.
findings.location: Section 4 — Reporter by accountId where permitted
findings.suggested_fix: In the reporter-set code, catch HTTPError and inspect the response body: only treat it as 'permission not granted' when the decoded JSON `errors` dict contains a `'reporter'` key (the standard Jira field-validation error shape). Re-raise or alert on bare 403 or a 400 with no `reporter` key in `errors`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the contradiction in §3: explicitly state whether a /user/search-resolved accountId is (a) used ephemerally for the current reconcile run only, or (b) written back to the identity store as a new mapping — and if (b), add the write/upsert function to §1 and its file to file_impact.
findings.finding: Section 3 uses the phrase 'bootstrapping the mapping', which implies writing a new {provider:"jira"} mapping entry back to the identity store, but §1 defines only read functions (resolve_mapping, jira_account_id) and no write/persist path appears anywhere in §1, the file_impact list, or the ACs — creating a contradiction between the What section's implied write-back and the read-only seam definition.
findings.suggested_fix: If ephemeral: change §3's wording from 'bootstrapping the mapping' to 'resolving the accountId for this reconcile run (ephemeral; not persisted to the identity store)'. If persistent: add an `upsert_mapping(identity_id, provider, external_id, *, repo_root=None) -> None` function to §1 and include the identity store file in file_impact.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in Section 3 whether the `/user/search`-resolved accountId is (a) written back to the identity entity (specify the event type, write module, and idempotency guard) or (b) used only ephemerally per pass (in which case rename it 'per-pass fallback' to avoid the misleading 'bootstrap' label and note it does not activate the Section 2 fast-path).
findings.finding: Section 3's /user/search 'bootstrap' mechanism never specifies whether the resolved accountId is written back (persisted) to the identity entity or used only ephemerally within the reconcile pass. If ephemeral, the feature is functionally identical to the existing validate_assignee_exists fallback and provides no durable bootstrap. If persistent, the write path, event type, idempotency guard, and responsible module are all absent from the plan.
findings.suggested_fix: If the intent is durable bootstrapping: add a sentence such as 'On a successful resolution, append a MAPPING_ADD event to the identity entity (or equivalent) so the resolved accountId enters the identity's `mappings` list and activates the Section 2 fast-path on subsequent passes; guard with a check for an existing `{provider:"jira"}` mapping to keep the write idempotent.' If the intent is only ephemeral: rename Section 3 'per-pass /user/search fallback' and note it supplements (not replaces) Section 2.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how `jira_account_id` is imported inside `_assignee_resolver` (a nested closure in `compute_outbound_mutations`) given that reconciler modules use spec-loading by file path and avoid top-level cross-package imports — either a lazy local import inside the closure, a lazy_load seam, or an injected callable parameter should be chosen and stated.
findings.finding: The plan asserts '`_assignee_resolver(assignee, jira_key)` (the existing local→accountId resolver)' can simply be modified to call `jira_account_id(assignee)` first, but does not acknowledge that `_assignee_resolver` is a **closure nested inside `compute_outbound_mutations`**, making the import of `jira_account_id` from `rebar._commands.identity` a cross-package boundary that the reconciler modules explicitly avoid (they use spec-loading / lazy-load by file path to prevent package-context failures). No import path or seam strategy is specified for this cross-boundary call.
findings.suggested_fix: Inject `jira_account_id` as an optional callable parameter into `compute_outbound_mutations` (defaulting to `None` to preserve backwards compatibility) and thread it into `_assignee_resolver` via closure capture — this avoids the cross-package import problem and keeps the module test-loadable without package context.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in Section 3 whether the `/user/search` bootstrap result is (a) transient — used only for the current reconcile cycle and discarded, or (b) persisted — written back to the identity entity's `mappings` for future cycles; state the default explicitly so the executor need not decide.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether the accountId resolved by the Section 3 `/user/search` bootstrap is persisted back into the identity entity's `mappings` or used only transiently for the current reconcile cycle — a real design decision an executor must make.
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to Section 3: 'The resolved accountId is used transiently for the current reconcile only and is NOT written back to the identity store; persistent mapping requires an explicit user action (out of scope here).' — or the inverse if persistence is intended.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in Section 1/2 how `jira_account_id` is imported into `outbound_differ.py` given the reconciler's `importlib`-by-path loading pattern — either acknowledge the direct `rebar._commands` import, or move the seam to a shared library module accessible without package context.
findings.finding: The plan calls `jira_account_id` from `outbound_differ.py` (inside the `_engine/rebar_reconciler` package), creating a new import from `rebar._commands.identity` into the reconciler layer. No existing reconciler module imports from `rebar._commands`; all cross-module loads use `lazy_load`/`importlib.util.spec_from_file_location`. This unenumerated architectural dependency is not acknowledged.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit observability requirement: each degradation path (bootstrap miss, no-mapping fallback to unassigned, reporter permission skip) must emit a structured log line at WARN level (at minimum) with the local assignee, the reason, and the affected ticket key, and the ACs must assert that the log/metric is emitted in the degradation test cases.
findings.finding: The plan specifies graceful-degradation for all three new failure paths (bootstrap miss, no-mapping assignee fallback, reporter permission skip) but does not require any structured observable signal — log line, metric increment, or trace annotation — for these degradation events, leaving operators unable to detect silent mis-assignments or skipped reporters in production.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Acknowledge and justify the `_commands` → `_engine` layer dependency (or relocate `resolve_mapping`/`jira_account_id` to a shared `src/rebar/identity/` module that both layers can import without inversion), and capture the architectural decision in an ADR or CLAUDE.md update.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a cross-layer import from `_commands/identity.py` into `_engine/rebar_reconciler/outbound_differ.py` (commands layer → engine layer), which is a layer-inversion coupling that is neither acknowledged, justified, nor mitigated via an interface or event boundary, and no ADR or design-doc update is called out.
findings.location: ## What, Section 2 / file_impact: outbound_differ.py calling jira_account_id from _commands/identity.py
findings.suggested_fix: Move `resolve_mapping` and `jira_account_id` to a provider-neutral `src/rebar/identity/resolution.py` module (no layer affiliation), imported by both `_commands/identity.py` (for CLI use) and `outbound_differ.py`, eliminating the layer inversion. Add a one-paragraph ADR noting the shared-module decision.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the required Jira permission for `/rest/api/3/user/search` (Browse Users global permission), add a probe/check or operator note confirming the service account has it, and distinguish the 403/empty-due-to-permissions path from the 'email not found' path in the degradation logging.
findings.finding: Section 3 introduces `/rest/api/3/user/search?query=<email>` as a new endpoint surface. This endpoint requires the 'Browse Users' global Jira permission (distinct from 'Assignable User' permission used by /assignable/search). A service account configured only for issue operations may lack Browse Users, causing every bootstrap call to silently return 0 results — indistinguishable from 'email not found'. The plan neither names this prerequisite nor verifies it applies to the existing credentials.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an explicit note: 'The /user/search bootstrap requires the Browse Users global Jira permission. If the service account lacks it, the call returns 403 (swallowed) or 0 results; log a one-time WARN distinguishing `HTTP 403 — Browse Users not granted, bootstrap skipped` from `0 results — email not found`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one concrete proving command per AC (e.g., `pytest tests/_commands/test_identity.py -k 'resolve_mapping'` for AC1, and equivalent paths for AC2/AC3), and add the test files to `file_impact`.
findings.finding: No concrete proving command (e.g., a `pytest` path or equivalent) is given for any acceptance criterion, leaving done-ness dependent on convention rather than a deterministic check an executor or reviewer can run.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to each AC a parenthetical like `(run: pytest tests/_commands/test_identity.py -k test_resolve_mapping)` and extend file_impact with e.g. `tests/_commands/test_identity.py`, `tests/_engine/rebar_reconciler/test_outbound_differ.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether the bootstrap accountId resolution is persisted to the identity store or used only in-memory; if persisted, specify that the write is idempotent (upsert semantics on `{provider, external_id}` key) and add an AC asserting no duplicate mapping is created on repeated bootstrap runs.
findings.finding: Section 3 states the best-effort `/user/search` bootstrap 'MAY resolve the accountId at reconcile time (bootstrapping the mapping)' but does not clarify whether the resolved accountId is persisted back to the identity store or used only in-memory for the current sync; if it IS persisted, write idempotency under concurrent or repeated reconcile runs is unspecified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the atomicity/idempotency contract for the Section 3 bootstrap write-back: define ownership (who may write), require a check-then-write guard (e.g., 'only persist if no {provider:"jira"} mapping already exists'), and note whether the identity store provides an atomic upsert or requires a lock.
findings.finding: The best-effort bootstrap in Section 3 ('bootstrapping the mapping' by writing a discovered accountId back into the identity store at reconcile time) has no specified atomicity, ownership, or idempotency guarantee. Two concurrent reconcile runs on the same identity could both query /user/search and both attempt to write the same accountId mapping, creating a TOCTOU race on the identity record.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a clause: 'The bootstrap write-back is a conditional upsert: persist the accountId mapping only if no {provider:"jira"} entry already exists in the identity's mappings (idempotent under retry). The write must be atomic with respect to the identity store's update primitive (e.g., optimistic lock / ETag check).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the RETIRE/invalidation path for stale mappings: either declare them out-of-scope with an explicit rationale (e.g., 'Jira accountIds are permanent and never recycled per Atlassian docs'), or specify a re-verification or expiry mechanism.
findings.finding: No RETIRE or invalidation path is specified for stale identity mappings (e.g., a Jira account is deprovisioned, merged, or rotated so the stored accountId becomes invalid). The plan specifies CREATE (ddbe) and READ (resolve_mapping/jira_account_id) but not UPDATE or RETIRE, so stale mappings can silently persist and cause mis-assignment indefinitely.
findings.suggested_fix: If Atlassian's accountId stability guarantee is the rationale, add a one-line note: 'Jira Cloud accountIds are stable and never recycled (per Atlassian docs), so no invalidation path is required.' This closes the lifecycle gap with a documented assumption rather than new code.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add an AC that covers the bootstrap write-back (asserting a mapping is persisted after a successful /user/search resolution) or explicitly demote the bootstrap to a session-only lookup (no persistence), and update the Scope 'In' list accordingly.
findings.finding: Section 3 introduces a write side-effect on the identity store (persisting a discovered Jira accountId as a new mapping entry) that is not reflected in the title, the stated goals, the Scope section's 'In' list, or any Acceptance Criterion. This is scope drift beyond 'outbound assignee/reporter' resolution, and no end-state-observable proof (AC or test) covers the bootstrap write-back.
findings.location: Section 3 — /user/search bootstrap; Scope section; Acceptance Criteria
findings.suggested_fix: If persistence is intended: add AC — 'When /user/search resolves an accountId for an identity with an email but no Jira mapping, the mapping is written back to the identity store (test: assert mapping present after reconcile with stubbed /user/search response).' If not: change 'bootstrapping the mapping' to 'resolving the accountId for this reconcile run only (not persisted)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the Section 3 bootstrap write-back is idempotent: before emitting the store event, check whether the identity already carries a `{provider:"jira"}` mapping and skip the write if one is present, so repeated reconcile runs cannot double-apply the same mapping.
findings.finding: The Section 3 best-effort bootstrap ('bootstrapping the mapping' by writing a discovered Jira accountId back into an identity's `mappings` at reconcile time) does not specify that the write-back is idempotent — i.e., the plan does not require a 'skip if mapping already present' guard before emitting the store event. On repeated reconcile runs the same `{provider:"jira", external_id:<accountId>}` could be appended multiple times to the same identity's `mappings` list, producing duplicate entries.
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC or implementation note: 'Before writing the bootstrapped accountId mapping, check the identity's existing `mappings` for an entry with `provider=="jira"`; skip the EDIT event if one is already present. This makes the bootstrap idempotent across reconcile retries.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the transport-security contract for the new `/user/search?query=<email>` bootstrap call: assert HTTPS-only (reject or warn on http:// jira_url), note that query-param PII (email) will appear in Jira server/proxy access logs, and document this as an accepted risk or replace with a POST/body parameter if the Jira API supports it.
findings.finding: The plan's Section 3 best-effort /user/search bootstrap sends a user email address as a URL query parameter (?query=<email>) with no declared TLS/transport-security contract, leaving PII exposure in proxy/server access logs undeclared.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a guard in the bootstrap path that validates `jira_url` starts with `https://` before issuing any query-string email lookup, and add a plan note acknowledging that email-in-query-string appears in server access logs (acceptable given single-tenant deployment, or mitigated by using the POST form of the user search endpoint if available).

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
